sentences.text: From the station, a peasant picked me up.
And he said, oh, I know you.
I used to come to your parents.
And I was drinking coffee with-- I was buying the salt and sugar, all these products that I needed.
I know you.
You can come over.
You stay in my house.
And we will arrange that you will go through the river, through the water.
We had to go from Sanok to our town, Lesko.
From Sanok-- Sanok was at the river.
But the river was not everywhere the same level.
Some were shallow, and some were very deep.
And this was already December, where some of, some of the parts were frozen.
So he said, I know which part.
And you are not the only one, there are a lot of people who are crossing the river.
We will bring you late at night to the river.
And don't worry, you will get home.
We stayed in his house until, probably-- it was already dark.
It might have been 6, after 6 o'clock.
We were in his house until about 8 o'clock.
There's a knock at the door.
And who comes?
The German come over.
And we were about five or six people.
I didn't know anyone else besides-- I mean, I, I had no connections with the other group.
But he took together, on the horse and buggy, everybody, so many people.
The Germans took us and from there, they threw us in somewhere into a basement.
Because there were no windows, and no light.
And I don't know how long we were there, because there was no daylight.
But approximately between 12 and 24 hours.
During that period of time, the Germans took out everybody for interrogation.
And I was also there.
So they were probably looking for dollars, so maybe other-- they were looking for smugglers.
But since I had these few items that I bought for the 200 zloty, they first, they took this away from me.
And then they told me to undress almost completely, to the point where there were-- if there was a little seam, they were trying on the seam if I don't have something sewn in.
And at that time, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] after they have taken away my items-- some of the personal belongings, too, but not too many, and they gave me back.
And they said at 4 o'clock in the morning, we will take you to the water.
And you behave quietly, because the Russians are on the other side.
If they catch you, they will either send you home, or they will shoot you.
So in a way, they took everything away.
But they were collaborating, apparently, with that man that they took us to a water, which was not above the knee.
The water was very cold.
It was already December.
But somehow I made it on the other side of the river-- on the side of the river where the Russians were already-- into fields, which were familiar to me.
Because as young children-- I was 12, 13 years old-- but we used to go very frequently to make gym there on the open fields, from school.
Also, before I was dating, but still occasionally I went out with a boy.
So we went there walking.
So I was familiar with these fields.
I crossed them over.
And it was approximately 6 o'clock in the morning, when I came to the first house on this western outskirt of our city.
I came there, and I knew it was a Jewish family.
I was familiar because their daughter went with me to school.
I knocked at the door.
And I said, I just crossed the river.
I'm frozen, I'm wet.
Could you let me in?
And they, of course, let me in.
And I dried up there.
And as soon as it got light, the daylight-- must have been 8 o'clock already-- one of their kids went to where we lived, which was almost on the other side of the city.
They went to my mother, to my parents, to tell them that I am in their home.
My parents did not expect me, because there was no, no communication whatsoever.
There was no telephone.
We could not even write letters at that time yet, from, from one zone to the other.
Of course, when I came home, my mother was extremely, extremely happy.
My parents-- I mean, my father, too, needless to say.
Father was a little bit heartbroken that I worked a few years and had some money accumulated that it would have been better, you know, perhaps, send the money before the war.
This sort of I remember, because we were struggling, I would say, financially.
Perhaps it would have been better.
But if I would have brought these items-- anyhow.
But we were very, very-- mother was very happy.
And father was happy that I came home intact, sort of.
When I came home at the beginning-- and this was in December, in 1940, 1939-- a lot of my friends who lived and were in Lesko, they had already all kinds of positions.
When I came, a lot of positions were already taken.
Still, so at the beginning, in order to help out the family-- father was orthodox.
He did not-- even though he was wearing gray clothes or-- but he wouldn't work on Saturday.
Mother was working as a, a, oh, what you call them in English?
You know, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] two-- a watchmaker, a watchmaker, in an office building, so nothing would be taken away.
And Mother also, also, Mother was at their time, taking in laundry from the Russian soldiers.
The laundry was washed in a laundry establishment.
But Mother was ironing the shirts, before they went to the German-- not to the German, excuse me, to the Russian soldiers.
So at the beginning, the first two or three weeks, I was helping mother.
I was-- not Mother, I was ironing in order-- because we needed something.
When we came there, bread was-- food was the biggest problem.
It was established already that they were bringing flour to our town, and the bakery was baking bread.

But they could not really-- since our town swell up with-- instead of 4,000 or 600, 6,000 people, we had, perhaps 15,000 or more from the all people who ran from the German territories to our, to our-- over.
And they got stuck there, until they decided what to do with themselves, where to go.
So of course, the food supply was very, very critical.
Bread, sometimes you had to go at 4 o'clock in the morning.
We had to stay in line to get one kilo, which was two pounds of bread.
And by the time you came close to the door, they said it's all sold out, you have to wait for the next, for the next batch.
So the food situation was very critical.
And since we were not so well off, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was, it was a problem.
So whatever we were making helped a little bit.
Since my mother was working, whoever was working later on was getting, let's say, a half a pound of bread daily.
You know, there was the stamp system.
But the Russians immediately made a high school in our town.
And since I had a pretty good schooling and a lot of courses I took privately-- like algebra, some sciences-- I went to the high school.
And immediately, I, I received-- not immediately, but let's say within two, three weeks-- I received a high school diploma.
At the same time, maybe perhaps, because our town was on the boundary, the Russians have established a very, I would say, wide health care system, considering the size of our town.
First of all, because it was on the boundary, there were a lot of Russian, Russian dignitaries.
Or they were on all the supervisory position in the Department of Health, in the Department of Commerce.
They were-- so because of that, was a high school right away.
There was a-- a hospital was made, which was taken away from nuns.
The nuns had sort of like a little clinic, a Catholic clinic.
They established there a hospital.
And there was another clinic.
So, where the clinic was previously, was established a hospital.
A clinic was opened where by coincidence, one of our relatives was the head doctor of the clinic.
And he was nice enough to give me a job, first in the clinic.
And then they opened a department-- at the health department, they opened a department of [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] preventive, to prevent illnesses, vaccination.
And they made me the head of that department.
And we had, in that department, two younger girls.
My cousins were working there.
They were mostly [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] given the job of going into the villages, to give vaccinations for diphtheria, dysentery, scarlet fever, mumps, and chicken pox, these.
So there was a crew.
And at that place, I was doing the statistical work, how much we do.
Also, if there were some infectious diseases, we had two men.
One was taking care of the horses-- horse and buggy, we didn't have cars at that time.
And one was doing, when there was a illness, he was doing the disinfection, like smoking up.
There was a quarantine, and after the quarantine-- so I was working at this department for quite some time.
And this has, again, given me more of a contact with the health department.
And I was also taking classes, all kinds of classes, from a student who came from, from a good, good-- it was more than a high school.
It was like a middle, a lower, I would say, a bachelor degree in, in here.
And I was taking classes in my spare time.
So my aim was to go in-- into nursing.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was there in the health department.
I was working until June 22, 1941.
This is when the war between the Germans and the Russians started.
And our city was on the boundary, so we knew it immediately.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] At that time, I was very friendly with one-- the head nurse in one of the clinics.
We had, at that time, a very big clinic, which was on a pretty high level, considering-- I am comparing to the size of our town from until the Russians came.
She was in the clinic.
She had a very good education from Krakow.
She was married to a-- her husband was born in our town.
And he had parents in our town.
Because of that, they were running from Krakow, and they remain in our town.
At the same time-- this friend's name was Irene-- and her mother and three sisters also came from near Krakow, from a town village, God, they were-- everybody was running.
When they came to Lesko, since she was in Lesko and she was the head nurse, they also stayed there.
Now this, I'm going back to about the, the time of 1940, I would say April 1940.
At that time, since our town was on the boundary, the Russians felt that the element which ran from the Germans-- a lot of them left family behind there.
And at one time, they said that people who want to go back to their previous homes, on the German territory, they could register.
And there's going to be an exchange of some Volksdeutsche from Russians-- anyhow, there was going to be an exchange between the Germans and the Russians.
But I don't know if this was a trick.
Or perhaps there was an exchange.
But the people who registered to go back to their home was 10 or 20 times bigger than the ones, perhaps, from the German territory to the Russians.
So one night, in April, approximately April 1940, in the middle of the night, at 12 o'clock-- and this was exactly, I would say, the methods of the Germans-- the Russians had this order.
They knocked at every door.
And they had a list, this and this and this was registered to go in the German.
Take your whatever you can take, your belonging.
That that time, not only from our town, from all towns in Zapad na Ukrajina.
This was the Polish territory, which was occupied-- sort of liberated or occupied by the Russians-- and these people were put on cattle trains, whatever was available.
And this is the element that was sent to Siberia.
And thanks to that, Stalin meant not to do a favor to the Jews.
But somehow it worked out that a lot of them survived.
And in 1946, they came on the Polish territory.
So there are a lot of Jews who were sent to Siberia and came back.
Now I will return to June, 1922-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] to June 22, 1942.
At that time, I was very close with that Irene.
Irene's mother and three sisters, since they registered, they were also sent to Siberia.
She had an old mother who was a diabetic.
And she was selling her insulin, because she didn't think that mother would ever be able to survive.
So I knew that her mother is somewhere deep in Russia.
I was very close with Irene.
The day when the war started, we were two more days, we were in the clinic.
There were two little injuries, but nothing connecting with shooting.
There was not really much.
There was not shooting, but the town was totally dead.
Everybody was in a terrible fear.
And there was talk.
Who's going to go away?
Who's going to-- the trains were not running anymore.
The first day, the Russian, the Russian dignitaries who were in our town took their wives and children and put them on the train and send them away.
So our station-- which was not in the middle of the city, it was maybe three kilometers away-- had only cattle trains.

But two days later, there was talk that they want to take the young people, the very, I would say, capable-- the ones who were working, the best element of our town-- that they are going away from our town, because it's dangerous.
And if you want to come with us, you can come with us.
It so happened that Irene, since we had a horse and buggy-- so she said, we'll take the horse and buggy.
Her husband went.
Her nephew and her niece went.
And when my mother heard that she is going and she knew how close we were, my mother was running out to look after me.
I wasn't even home at that time.
She said, look, Irene is leaving our town.
Since we're on the boundary, I would like you to go with her.
This was-- this were my mother's words.
I would have never gone away from home, because I came home to stay with my parents.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So first, I said I have to go and ask Irene if she wants to take me.
I was eight years younger than Irene.
Irene was already a married lady.
And she said, yes, of course.
There is room on the horse and buggy.
So I came home, and I asked my brother, would you like to go along?
I didn't want to go along.
Well, I don't know.
It was a mistake on my part, because he was the next after me.
So the parents were really left with the youngest three children.
And then there was another brother, David, who was rather sickly.
When my brother, the older brother said, I don't know, maybe, yes, maybe no.
So Mother said, take David.
And I was afraid.
I was myself-- I knew that Irene is sort of like the chaperone, my-- so I was afraid to take David.
Maybe if he would have gone, he would have survived, too.
But I said, no, maybe David should remain home.
And then, when we were gathering a few of our items and we came home, my uncle, my mother's brother, lived in the same house.
We had like a two-story house.
He lived in the downstairs apartment.
And he sees me getting together things with my brother.
He said, where are you going?
So I said, my mother said we should go away, because Irene goes, and she has a horse and buggy.
So we'll go away from our town.
So he said, you know what, take Rachel along.
Rachel was younger than my brother David.
But I didn't have the guts to say no, I don't want to.
So Rachel came along, and Rachel survived, too.
Thanks to God, the three of us survived.
Anyhow,
We went on the horse and buggy.
And he started to drive, until halfway, in a open field, again, the same thing.
A German plane comes and-- [GUN NOISES] starts to shoot.
So we jumped off from the horse and buggy and into the fields.
There were a lot of trenches, to make the water run off from the roads because it's a hilly region.
And at that time, this was June.
So the wheat was high.
So we went, and we fell into these ditches.
So somehow, none of us got killed.
When we came back to the horse and buggy, that man, a Gentile who was-- he was working.
And this is why he came with us when we said-- when Irene said to us, come, let's go.
He said, look, you are afraid for the Germans.
If you run away and they shoot on you, and your life is in danger, so it's your prerogative.
I have my wife there, and my children in Lesko.
I have nothing to be afraid of.
He turned around the horse and buggy and left us in the middle of the road.
This was about, probably, 30, 40 kilometers from a town which is called Sambor.
While we had no alternative, we started to walk.
We were all young.
I mean, Irene and her husband were, let's say, 28, 30, the most.
We were walking.
While we were walking, we see the Russians, who had two, I think, cars, one pickup, and one car.
They took along the cream of the crop.
They told them if you want to come with us, don't take too much, but leave a little package on the pickup.
And whoever was very tired went on the pickup.
And the others, we were walking.
And while they were walking, we saw them on the road.
So my brother said right away-- he knew a lot of his friends from, from the trade, trade workshop.
They were making furniture there.
He said, look, my friends are there.
So let's join them already.
So we were about 30 kilometers from Sambor.
And we walked to Sambor, where these Russian, you know, the bosses, took us into a school.
They told us to make ourselves comfortable.
We all lie down on the floor.
There were no beds or no place where to sleep.
But to rest on the floor and put a little knapsack under our heads.
And then, two or three hours later, they made a meeting.
And they told us, look, we came here.
We thought that we could stop here and start working here.
But the Germans are going at a very fast pace.
So we decided to go further.
There are trains here in Sambor.
We will put you on the trains.
When we asked them, where will we go, how far will we go, they said, we don't know ourselves.
Our wives and children left, and we also don't know if they arrived home or not.
This was their answer.
They gave everybody 50 ruble.
We should have something, because we had absolutely no money.
I was, at that time, hysterical.
Because I knew that this was like the end of seeing my family.
I had a feeling.
But my brother, who was very reluctant to go from home, here, since he saw some of his friends, he said no.
Since we came already here, and they are-- they want to send us deeper, we should go already deeper.
It makes no sense to remain here in a city that we don't know anybody.
This Irene knew a doctor.
He was the head of the clinic and the head of the hospital, since she was the head nurse.
His name was Dr.
[NON-ENGLISH].
He is himself Ukrainian, but a very, very peculiar man, a man who devoted his entire life to humanity, to helping sick people, without taking money.
He was able to go 30, 40 kilometers into the mountains to help a woman who give birth, because the birth was in danger.
And he was considered a very odd-- they call it a [NON-ENGLISH], a peculiar man.
But he, he was a bachelor.
And Irene, Irene was married, but apparently he sympathized her.
He liked her very much.
She was a extraordinary, lovely person.
This Dr.
[NON-ENGLISH] was not in our town at the time when the war started.
He was Sambor, where we had a brother.
So Irene said, instead of staying in this school on the floor, I will go where Dr.
[NON-ENGLISH] is with his brother.
And she was there with her husband, her nephew, and her niece.
But at the same time, when she heard-- she knew where in the school we are-- she came to see me.
So I told her, you know Irene, we are going to be sent somewhere on the Russian territory, possibly that very deep.
So her words to me were, look, my mother and the three sisters are in Siberia.
If it were up to me, I would come here and go together with you and everybody else.
But I don't know what my husband will say, and the nephew, because they have their parents and their family in Lesko.
But I will talk it over.
And I hope that they will decide, and I will join you.
Well, they put us on the cattle trains.
And in the middle of the night, we started.
I was sitting.
It was a beautiful night in, in June.
I looked, and I looked, looked, if Irene is coming with her husband and with her nephew.
But they didn't come.
Irene, her husband, her nephew remain there in Sambor.
And we were sent on to the Russian territory.
I want to just mention about Irene.
She stayed there as soon as the German went.
Sambor, of course, was not destroyed.
You know, the Polish were-- the Polish people, Polish soldiers were running, the army was running very deep.
And the German came in without any resistance.
And after a week, Irene, her husband, the nephew and niece decided to go back to Lesko from Sambor.
And when they came to a town, which is about 15 kilometer from our town, the town is called Ustrzyki.
And they slept over in, in Ustrzyki, to find out how the situation is in Lesko.
Because Lesko was on the river San.
It was more dangerous, they were not-- they wanted to find out how the story is.
In the middle of the night, there's a knock at the door.
They took out the two men-- Irene's husband and Irene's nephew-- and the owner of the person where they were sleeping.
And without any reasons, they were shot to death.
By Germans, by Germans.
This is according to Irene's-- Irene was hidden by Dr.
[NON-ENGLISH] later on.
She was in Lesko, in our town.
She was hidden by Dr.
[NON-ENGLISH] together with two of her friends, who were also professionals.
He really offered his life.
He paid eight-- that she was helping them.
And while the three girls survived hidden in a hospital on an attic, where he had a big sign, that this is the epidemiology apartment, nobody should come here, they three survived.
And the two of them were on very high position in Israel.
And Irene survived, and she also became-- she was a nurse.
And she was a nurse-- she later came to the United States.
To tell you, she married Dr.
[NON-ENGLISH].
He was promising her that he would come to United States.
But he was, he, he always promised, but he didn't come.
He came for a visit.
But would you imagine that Irene's mother and three sisters survived?
And they came back.
And the three sisters are in United States.
The mother died in our town, Lesko.
Because she was too sick to travel, she died there.
She's the only Jewish lady who died already after the war, in 19-- beginning of 19, end of 19-- probably, 1946.
Now to go back-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] where did I stop about?
So anyhow, from Sambor.
I will come back to Irene, because later we met after the war.
When, when I came back from the hospital.
OK.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] These trains took us to deep Russian territory, near Rostov.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They settled us not in a city but in a collective farm, which was called a kolkhoz.
In the kolkhoz, all the girls or boys-- nobody was given office jobs.
Whoever was there was working on the field.
Yeah.
